BOISE, July 29.-Charles A..
Meyer, president of the miners fed
Grafton, was released from prison
this morning on ball.


George A. Pettibone is still he'd
and the prosecution announces that
both will be prosecuted notwith-
standing Haywood's acquittal. Gov
enor Gooding is especially en-
phatic in declaring that the prose-
cutlon will go on.


Haywood is preparing to leave
for Denver with his family. He is
overcome today with congratula-
tons from all over the country.


With the stiffness of the tomb
prevailing in the courtroom, and
the nerves Of those most closely
concerned almost at the breaking
point, the jury at o'clock yester-
day morning returned a verdict Of
not guilty in the famous trial Of
William D. Haywood, accused of
completely in the assassination of
Ex-Governor Steunenberg.


The moment the words were pro.
nonuced Haywood seemed to under
70 transformation. He was plainly
strong and confident when he en-
tered the courtroom, but the ter
rible strain had told on him heavily
and his eye never left the jurymen
after they slowly filed into the
room.


Amidst the flood of congratula-
tions that poured over the acquitted
man Judge Wood formally dis
charged the defendant and the jury.
Haywood left the room as soon as
he could and went to the Jail,
where he told his colleagues, Moy-
err and Pettibone, and shook hands
all round. Then he hurried to ST.
Luke's hospital, where his aged
mother was suffering critically
from worry. His greeting was
most affecting and the news of her
sons acquittal had a marvellous
effect on the stricken woman.


Then Haywood hurried home to
the invalid wife and little daugh-
tiers who awaited him in the cot
tage they have occupied since COM
inc to Boise for the trial. That
meeting can be poorly imagined and
cannot be described. For nearly 2
years the family had been separat-
ed, Haywood having been taken
from their home at Denver at
night and kept in prison under a
charge that meant death II sus-
tanned.


From statements given out by
jurymen the first ballot taken after
Thomas Cess had been elected fore
man stood 8 for acquittal, for
conviction and 2 blank, the latter
being cast by Foreman Cess and
A. P. Burns. On the second ballot
Cess voted for acquittal. Burns
followed on the next ballot and
there the vote stood for hours,
Thomas Powell and S. d. Gilman
holding out for conviction in some
form.


Shortly before 4 a. M, Sunday,
Powell decided to vote for acquit-
tai. Gilman had meanwhile gone
to sleep and was not reused until
o'clock when another ballot was
asked for. Gilman then cast his
ballot In favor Of Haywood and
Judge Wood was notified that an
agreement had been reached.


Judge Wood responded promptly
and was in his courtroom before
the attorneys interested arrived
Haywood was also on hand before
Darrow came and told those who
spoke to him that he was feeling
we'll and hopeful.


Worn by the weeks Of trial and
the strain of 81 hours in the sitting
jury room wrestling with the ver-
diet, the Jury filed slowly into the
